section.name: health-check-structure
section.summary: Health-check script structure, coverage requirements, resource thresholds, and exit code conventions for solo-developer VPS platforms.

rule.statement: A health-check script must cover all four layers: infrastructure (Docker), application (systemd), endpoints (HTTP), and system resources.
rule.rationale: A service can be "active" in systemd but returning 500s; an endpoint check catches what systemd status misses. Resource checks catch slow degradation before hard failures.

rule.statement: Use exit codes: 0 = healthy, 1 = warnings, 2 = errors. Never exit 0 when checks fail.
rule.rationale: Cron jobs and monitoring wrappers test exit codes to decide whether to send alerts. A silent non-zero exit from a health check defeats its purpose.

rule.statement: Print OK/WARN/FAIL prefix on every check line so output is machine-parseable without regex.
rule.rationale: Downstream scripts and log scanners can grep for FAIL without parsing natural language sentences.

list.item: Infrastructure: Docker daemon active, each container running (postgres, redis, rabbitmq)
Application: each systemd user service active (nero-core, channel-web, channel-tg, web, beat)
Endpoints: HTTP health endpoint returns 2xx, frontend returns 2xx
Resources: disk percent, memory percent, CPU load vs. core count, swap usage
Swap: warn when more than 50% of swap is used

example.code:
# Wrong: always exits 0, alert wrappers never trigger ./health-check.sh || true
# Wrong: no resource checks, disk fills silently check_service "Docker" "systemctl is-active docker" echo "Done" exit 0
example.note: Health checks that always exit 0 are worse than no health check — they create false confidence.

section.reference: Comprehensive health-check covering Docker, systemd user services, HTTP endpoints, disk/memory/CPU/swap

section.name: cron-integration
section.summary: How to wire health-check scripts into cron for automated alerting.

rule.statement: Run health checks with an alert-on-failure wrapper in cron, not by piping directly to Telegram inside the check script.
rule.rationale: Separating health-check logic from alerting logic lets you run the same check manually without triggering alerts.

example.code:
# Run health check every 5 minutes; alert if non-zero exit */5 * * * * /usr/local/bin/health-check.sh || /usr/local/bin/notify-telegram.sh "Health check FAILED on $(hostname)"
# FLOW hourly monitor (alert-only, no noise on success) 0 * * * * /home/nero/workspace/scripts/flow-hourly.sh >> /var/log/nero-flow.log 2>&1
